title: Representation of data
summary: This lesson covers the fundamental techniques for representing and summarising data as required for Cambridge A-Level Probability & Statistics 1. It focuses on constructing and interpreting stem-and-leaf diagrams, histograms, and cumulative frequency graphs. Key skills include calculating estimates for the mean and standard deviation from grouped data, and determining the median and interquartile range to compare distributions effectively.
learningObjectives: Represent numerical data using stem-and-leaf diagrams, histograms, and cumulative frequency graphs.
Calculate estimates for the mean and standard deviation from grouped data.
Estimate the median, quartiles, and interquartile range from a cumulative frequency graph.
Compare and contrast data sets using appropriate measures of central tendency and spread.

simpleExplanation.title: Decoding Data Displays
simpleExplanation.summary: We turn long lists of numbers into clear pictures like histograms and box plots. These visuals help us quickly grasp the data's story by showing its centre, spread, and shape.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you've just bought a massive box of assorted chocolates. To understand what you have, you wouldn't just stare at the pile. You'd group them by type (caramel, nut, dark, milk), count how many of each you have, and maybe find the 'average' chocolate. Representing data is the same: we group, count, and summarise numbers to make sense of them, finding the average value (mean) or the middle value (median).
steps: First, group raw data into classes. For a histogram, calculate frequency density (frequency divided by class width) to determine the bar height, ensuring area represents frequency.
To estimate the mean from grouped data, use the midpoint of each class as a representative value. The formula is the sum of (midpoint × frequency) divided by the total frequency.
Plot a cumulative frequency curve (ogive) to find the median. Locate the position of the median at n/2 on the vertical axis, then read across to the curve and down to the horizontal axis.
Finally, measure the data's spread. Calculate the interquartile range (IQR) from the ogive or use your calculator for standard deviation to compare how consistent or varied different datasets are.

flashcards.front: What is a key difference between a histogram and a bar chart?
flashcards.back: A histogram is for continuous data with no gaps between bars, and the area of each bar is proportional to frequency. A bar chart is for discrete or categorical data and has gaps between bars.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for Frequency Density?
flashcards.back: Frequency Density = Frequency / Class Width. This is used as the vertical axis for a histogram with unequal class widths.
The exact values that separate one class from the next in grouped continuous data. For example, for classes 10-14 and 15-19, the boundary is 14.5.

flashcards.front: How do you find the mid-point of a class interval?
flashcards.back: Add the upper and lower class boundaries and divide by 2. For a class 10 ≤ x < 20, the midpoint is (10 + 20) / 2 = 15.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for the estimated mean of grouped data?
flashcards.back: $\bar{x} \approx \frac{\sum fx}{\sum f}$, where $x$ is the mid-point of each class and $f$ is the frequency of that class.

flashcards.front: What is a cumulative frequency graph also known as?
How do you plot a cumulative frequency graph?
flashcards.back: Plot the cumulative frequency on the y-axis against the upper class boundary of each class on the x-axis. The first point is (lower boundary of first class, 0).

flashcards.front: How do you estimate the median from a cumulative frequency graph?
flashcards.back: Find the position of the (n/2)th value on the cumulative frequency axis, read across to the curve, and then read down to the horizontal axis.

flashcards.front: How do you find the Interquartile Range (IQR) from a cumulative frequency graph?
flashcards.back: Find the values for the upper quartile (Q3 at 3n/4) and lower quartile (Q1 at n/4) from the graph. The IQR is Q3 - Q1.

flashcards.front: What are the five key values summarised in a box-and-whisker plot?
flashcards.back: Minimum value, Lower Quartile (Q1), Median (Q2), Upper Quartile (Q3), and Maximum value.

flashcards.front: Common Trap: When calculating the mean from a grouped frequency table, what value must you use for 'x' in Σfx?
flashcards.back: You must use the class mid-point. Using the lower/upper boundary or the class width is a common error.

flashcards.front: When comparing two datasets, what two features must you always comment on?
flashcards.back: You must compare a measure of central tendency (e.g., median or mean) and a measure of spread (e.g., IQR or standard deviation).

faq.q: What is the main difference between a histogram and a bar chart?
faq.a: A histogram is used for continuous data grouped into intervals, and the bars must touch. The area of each bar represents frequency. A bar chart is for discrete or categorical data, and the bars have gaps between them. The height of each bar represents frequency.

faq.q: Why do we use midpoints to estimate the mean of grouped data?
faq.a: When data is grouped, we lose the original individual values. The midpoint of a class is our best estimate for the average value of all the data points within that class. By using it, we can calculate a weighted average that provides a good approximation of the true mean.

faq.q: When should I use the median/IQR versus the mean/standard deviation to compare data?
faq.a: Use the median and IQR when the data is skewed or contains significant outliers. The median is a 'resistant' measure, meaning it's not heavily influenced by extreme values. Use the mean and standard deviation when the data is roughly symmetrical and free from major outliers, as they use all data points in their calculation and are often more powerful.

faq.q: On a cumulative frequency graph, why do we plot points at the upper class boundary?
faq.a: The cumulative frequency for a given class represents the total count of all data points up to and including that class. Therefore, this total is reached at the very end of the class interval, which is the upper class boundary. The graph starts at the lower boundary of the first class with a cumulative frequency of zero.

sections.content: In statistics, we are often confronted with large sets of raw data. Simply looking at a list of numbers makes it difficult to spot patterns, trends, or significant features. This topic introduces the essential methods for organising, summarising, and visually representing data. Mastering these techniques will allow you to extract meaningful information and make valid comparisons between different datasets, a core skill in statistics.

A stem-and-leaf diagram is a simple but effective way to display quantitative data while retaining the original data values. It separates each data point into a 'stem' (the leading digit or digits) and a 'leaf' (the final digit). The stems are listed vertically, and the leaves are listed horizontally next to their corresponding stem. It's crucial to include a key to explain how to read the diagram. For comparing two datasets, a back-to-back stem-and-leaf diagram can be used, with a central stem and leaves for each dataset branching out on either side.

items: Shows the shape of the distribution (e.g., symmetry, skewness).
Data is ordered, which makes finding the median and quartiles straightforward.

sections.content: When data is continuous and has been grouped into class intervals, a histogram is the appropriate diagram. Unlike a bar chart, there are no gaps between the bars. A crucial feature of a histogram is that the area of each bar is proportional to the frequency of that class. If the class widths are all equal, the heights of the bars are proportional to the frequencies. However, if the class widths are unequal, you must calculate and plot frequency density on the vertical axis.

To estimate the mean from a grouped frequency table, we assume that all the data values within a class are evenly distributed and can be represented by the mid-point of that class. We then calculate a weighted average.

sections.question:
The times, $t$ minutes, taken by 80 people to complete a crossword puzzle are summarised in the table below. 

| Time (t mins) | Frequency | 
|---|---|
| 10 ≤ t < 15 | 10 |
| 15 ≤ t < 25 | 30 |
| 25 ≤ t < 35 | 25 |
| 35 ≤ t < 50 | 15 |

(i) Draw a histogram to represent this information. 
(ii) Calculate an estimate for the mean time taken.
sections.solution:
(i) First, we need to calculate the class widths and frequency densities for the unequal class intervals.

| Time (t mins) | Class Width | Frequency | Frequency Density (f/cw) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 ≤ t < 15 | 5 | 10 | 10/5 = 2.0 |
| 15 ≤ t < 25 | 10 | 30 | 30/10 = 3.0 |
| 25 ≤ t < 35 | 10 | 25 | 25/10 = 2.5 |
| 35 ≤ t < 50 | 15 | 15 | 15/15 = 1.0 |

The histogram is drawn with 'Time (t mins)' on the horizontal axis and 'Frequency Density' on the vertical axis. The bars have widths 5, 10, 10, 15 and heights 2.0, 3.0, 2.5, 1.0 respectively. There are no gaps between the bars.

(ii) To estimate the mean, we need the mid-point ($x$) of each class.

| Time (t mins) | Mid-point (x) | Frequency (f) | fx |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 ≤ t < 15 | 12.5 | 10 | 125 |
| 15 ≤ t < 25 | 20 | 30 | 600 |
| 25 ≤ t < 35 | 30 | 25 | 750 |
| 35 ≤ t < 50 | 42.5 | 15 | 637.5 |

Total frequency, $\sum f = 10+30+25+15 = 80$.
Total $fx$, $\sum fx = 125 + 600 + 750 + 637.5 = 2112.5$.

Estimated Mean, $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum fx}{\sum f} = \frac{2112.5}{80} = 26.40625$.

So, the estimated mean time is **26.4 minutes** (to 3 s.f.).

sections.content: Cumulative Frequency, Quartiles, and Box Plots

A cumulative frequency graph, or ogive, is a running total of the frequencies. It is plotted using the upper class boundary of each interval against the cumulative frequency. These graphs are extremely useful for estimating values that relate to position in the dataset, most notably the median ($Q_2$), the lower quartile ($Q_1$), and the upper quartile ($Q_3$). The median is the value of the middle data point, while the quartiles divide the data into four equal parts.

The difference between the upper and lower quartiles is the Interquartile Range (IQR), a robust measure of spread that is not affected by extreme outliers. A box-and-whisker plot provides a concise visual summary of the data using five key values: the minimum value, $Q_1$, the median, $Q_3$, and the maximum value. They are excellent for comparing the distributions of two or more datasets side-by-side.

sections.question:
The cumulative frequency graph shows the heights, in cm, of 120 plants.

(A cumulative frequency graph is shown, starting at (0,0), passing through (10, 20), (20, 70), (30, 105), (40, 120). The x-axis is 'Height (cm)' and the y-axis is 'Cumulative Frequency'.)

(i) Use the graph to estimate the median height.
(ii) Estimate the interquartile range of the heights.
(iii) It was found that the shortest plant was 5 cm and the tallest was 38 cm. Draw a box-and-whisker plot to represent the data.
sections.solution:
(i) The total number of plants is $n=120$. The median is the value of the $(n/2)$th plant, which is the 60th plant.
Reading from 60 on the cumulative frequency axis across to the curve and down to the height axis gives an estimated median.
Median ($Q_2$) ≈ **18 cm**.

(ii) The lower quartile ($Q_1$) is at the $(n/4)$th position = 120/4 = 30th plant.
Reading from 30 on the y-axis gives $Q_1 \approx$ **12 cm**.
The upper quartile ($Q_3$) is at the $(3n/4)$th position = 3*120/4 = 90th plant.
Reading from 90 on the y-axis gives $Q_3 \approx$ **25 cm**.

Interquartile Range (IQR) = $Q_3 - Q_1 \approx 25 - 12 = 13$ cm.
So, the estimated IQR is **13 cm**.

(iii) We have the five-number summary:
Minimum = 5 cm
Lower Quartile ($Q_1$) = 12 cm
Median ($Q_2$) = 18 cm
Upper Quartile ($Q_3$) = 25 cm
Maximum = 38 cm

A box-and-whisker plot is drawn with a horizontal scale from 0 to 40. 
A box is drawn from $Q_1=12$ to $Q_3=25$. 
A vertical line is drawn inside the box at the median, $M=18$. 
Whiskers are drawn from the box to the minimum value (5) and the maximum value (38).

sections.content: When asked to compare two distributions, you must give two distinct points of comparison. One must be a measure of central tendency (e.g., 'The median time for Group A was higher than for Group B, suggesting they were slower on average'). The second must be a measure of spread (e.g., 'The IQR for Group A was smaller, suggesting their times were more consistent'). Simply stating the values is not enough; you must interpret what they mean in the context of the question.

items.label: Cambridge International AS & A Level Mathematics 9709

steps.caption: Grouped data: class boundaries, midpoints, frequency density.
Mean of grouped data: Σfx / Σf with midpoints.
Median class from cumulative frequency curve.
Measure spread: range, IQR, standard deviation (calculator).
steps.embedHint: Compare two distributions by centre and spread.
